item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really well-paced, getting weirder precisely where it needs to by ditching the D's and going fully self-aware. The cat is brilliant. The ending to all this doofiness actually manages to be poignant. I just really, really admire the structure and concept overall.

I guess it's a little odd that the Foundation just casually rolls with changes to relationships and standing, as that seems like something they'd get extremely antsy about after seeing it happen a few times and would start laying into the amnestics. But, at the same time, there's an argument in favor of them not sweating folks, especially loyal folks (a point specifically mentioned here) experiencing life changes due to anomalies, because how could you do this for a living and expect it not to happen?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The attitude of the Foundation is waaaaay too relaxed in this entry. They do not release D-class, they definitely do not release D-class who have been exposed to paranormal cognitohazards. Most of all, they would never let Foundation personnel screw around with cognitohazardous skips for recreational purposes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was quite a refreshing piece. Fairly distinct without being incoherent or feeling out of place with the Foundation. Upvote for sure.

I hated, hated writing this thing. It was vast and horrible and took a long time. It took so much out of me, and then when I finally posted it was at a really silly time so nobody read it and it got stuck on +18 for ages. It took my skips quite a long time to recover from the psychological blow, as I ended up writing a lot of half-baked, rather bad stuff until a month or two ago.

And now it's here. It probably wouldn't have got this high without Modern Erasmus and Shaggydredlocks mentioning it on Reddit a lot, so thanks to them too. I feel oddly vindicated after the initial reaction, and I'm very glad so many people liked this silly skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You have earned yourself an upvote and a standing ovation. Good show.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Safavi and Wong hooking up after the LOVE section elicited a big "AAWWW!" from me, probably the sweetest thing I've read on this site. This entire skip is rather beautiful, to be honest, and with a good share of humour too.

+1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ugh god

Entries like this are my bread and butter
I love this!

One thought:
SCP Cross-testing results :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't even be irrational right.

Changed to a novote. :B

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You seem to have upvoted by mistake, so you might want to fix that :p.

And while we do indeed have 1981-J, that was very different in style and tone. As was the other musical skip recently posted (Kirby's one, I think), leaving this one to still be distinct in form. But, fair enough, each to their own :p if it didn't work for you it didn't work for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's really unfortunate that I'm finding this nearly a year after it was posted, because my thoughts are thus:

- We already have a "Don't Stop Believin'" -J, unless I'm totally off the mark

- We already have a skip about people getting sucked into stage productions

That second one is wrong, though, because the skip I'm thinking of was posted earlier this year. If anything, that one's derivative of this. But I can't get over the fact that I've seen this before, and the second version is a lot less silly in presentation.

I just want to be clear that I am downvoting for entirely subjective and irrational reasons. Won't harm the article's standing in the slightest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since the authorship is no longer clear from this: this skip was written by me, Tufto, on my first account prior to my deleting it. You can find more of my stuff here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Author here- I deleted my original account a while ago for various reasons, and made a new one shortly after. Glad you liked it :).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is by far my most loved SCP to read!

I especially loved the part when Dr. Westwood was being forced into a meta rom-com and then realized it at the end and being really embarrassed.

In my mind the command reaction would be:

Command: Would you like to view the recorded footage of you singing back then?
Westwood: Shut up

Also it's nice change of pace seeing highest clearance officer in the entire facility singing and dancing like a Disney princess

+1

(Also it seems that the author's account is gone, anybody knows why?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've appeared from more than a year of silence to tell you how happy this article made me. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Goddamn this is awesome. And wholesome indeed.
One of the easiest +1 I have ever given.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: havent even read it yet but a quick glance tells me that its going to be awesome.

item.title: fuckign great
item.content:encoded: my +1 was decided when westwood made a meta-rant and was solidified when Amir found love

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd suggest you include that bit of info at the begonning of a log :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Surreptitiously attached to the people sent in; probably on their collars or over their ears :p.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not at all; I briefly considered doing something like that myself. But I don't think a log for a skip this obscure will get a great deal of attention.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is quite possibly one of the most enjoyable SCPs I've ever read. My only question is this: Where were the cameras?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Will you be mad if I make a collaborative log?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for getting my fact about the jazz singer in there an overall excellent article - good characters, exploration logs and a nice base concept. Good work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Long, but worth the read. The narrative was surprisingly capturing for how long it was, and the characters are well thought-out. This is a concept definitely requiring an exploration log, and you executed it very well.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad to see it work out for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was quite an engaging piece, despite its length. I halfway expected (given the format and full context of each iteration) for things to get dull/repetitive, but I was pleasantly surprised at how well you managed to milk this at every turn - I really cannot pick out a favorite log. Bonus points for not skimping on the musical numbers!

+1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bravo! Next stop, Broadway!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pretty engaging to read, nice work.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ahh, thanks; shall fix.

Glad you liked it :).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow. Huge, genre-bending, sad, heartwarming, and well executed. This is by far my favorite piece of yours so far, bravo! Just so you know there are a few spacing errors still around, and one case where a speaker wasn't bolded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
With thanks to those who read over all/part of it:

Vacuum
Meta
Ninevolt
Icepick/Mortos

Now, I was planning to get more crit, and some people had indeed offered to critique it. I do apologise to those people for somewhat-prematurely posting; but this draft was taking up far too much of my time, and I think it's best if I just post it, get it out of my head and then take a short (i.e. a few days to a few weeks) break from this site. I'm posting this more to get it off my mind than for any other reason. And because I think getting anyone else to wade through this mess to critique it is just cruel at this stage. Is is 9672 words long, after all.

With that said, I do hope you all enjoy it :) I have put far more work into this than I should have. But if you downvote, then I completely understand; it's long, sprawling and corny.

SPOILERS for those who don't want to read the whole thing
SPOILERS for those who don't want to read the whole thing

Flawed pointed out that I should probably add a summary, as it's very long and people might not want to read 10,000ish words of this stuff :p.

SCP-XXXX is a train station that only appears at midnight The train within it takes people to several extradimensional train stations; behind each one is a cinematic scene which the subject gets drawn into as the protagonist. When put together, the scenes form a traditional cinematic narrative: of someone who leaves the countryside to arrive at the bright lights of the city, runs into hardship, meets a love interest, gets a "big break" by attaining success in their chosen field, confesses their feelings to the love interest, has a happy climax, and then returns by train to the original station. These scripts all fulfil some form of fantasy/ retell a happy memory in the subject's life.

I've used this to tell 9 stories:

  1. The story of D-3150, a failed ska musician who makes it big in Manchester.
  2. The story of D-2072, a noir fan whose script involves her proving her own innocence.
  3. The story of D-3150, a woman who murdered her abusive boyfriend, and whose script involves her living a fantasy where she is innocent.
  4. Dr. Westwood, a cynical researcher who disdains SCP-XXXX and whose love interest is as meta-minded as she is, but who gets forced into a romantic-comedy script against her wishes.
  5. Dr. Montague, a Cogwork Orthodoxy defector whose script involves him leaving a world of clockwork into a strange version of the Foundation with buildings constructed entirely of wood and greenery.
  6. Dr. Safavi, whose script concerns his infatuation with a colleage, Dr. Wong, and is set in Safavid Isfahan.
  7. Skimbleshanks, Dr. Perry's cat. Does cat things.
  8. Dr. Perry, who has a v. traditional "Country girl goes to Hollywood and makes it big in acting" story, the archetype upon which all the scripts were vaguely based. Gives a musical number about the joy and regret of fantasies.

It's very long, but I don't think there's anything I really could have cut.
